coredata.dc:title: Nanocomposite coating based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots in extending shelf life of fresh-cut pears from the perspective of microorganism
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, WeiYu
Wang, Yihao
Li, Wenxiang
Cheng, Fansheng
Xu, XingFeng
Sun, Yanan
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped quantum carbon dots were prepared based on Ethylenediaminetetra acetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-Na2) and citric acid, characterized for their structures and properties, and blended with chitosan to make nanocomposite coating film applied in the preservation of fresh-cut pears. The results showed that the PER-CDs (Pleurotus eryngii by-products carbon quantum dots), EDTANa2-doped PER carbon quantum dots (EPER-CDs), EDTANa2 and citric acid doped PER carbon quantum dots (E/NPER-CDs) prepared by hydrothermal method successfully introduced N–H groups on the surfaces, and the doped carbon dots showed good antibacterial on Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus and antioxidant properties. At the end of storage (5 d), the total bacterial count of fresh-cut pears coated with CS (chitosan)/2%E/NPER-CDs was 5.59 log CFU/g significantly lower than that of the control group (7.29 log CFU/g). The E/NPER-CDs inhibited the accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), delayed degradation of total phenol, ascorbic acid and glutathione, as well as significantly reduced the activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), polyphenol oxidase (POD), peroxidase (PPO), and ascorbic acid oxidase (APX) to achieve the effect of enhancing the quality of fresh-cut pears during storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen doped
ROS metabolism
Antibacterial
Fresh-cut pears

Determination of antioxidant substances in fresh-cut pears

Determination of enzyme activities related to ROS metabolism
Application of N-doped CDs/chitosan coating in fresh-cut pear
Changes in the appearance of fresh-cut pears
Changes in the total bacterial count of fresh-cut pears
Effect of different treatments on antioxidant content of fresh-cut pears
Effect of different treatments on H2O2 and O2 ·-content of fresh-cut pears
The enzymes activities related to ROS metabolism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of PER-CDs (b) TEM image of EPER-CDs (c) TEM image of E/NPER-CDs (d) FTIR spectrum of CDs (e) UV absorption spectra of CDs (f) XPS spectrum of CDs (g) XRD image of CDs (h) Fluorescence spectra of PER-CDs with different excitation wavelengths (i) Fluorescence spectra of EPER-CDs with different excitation wavelengths (j) Fluorescence spectra of E/NPER-CDs with different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Scavenging ability of three carbon quantum dots on DPPH, hydroxyl radicals(·OH) and superoxide anion(O2 ·-) (b) Inhibition rate of E. coli by different concentrations of carbon quantum dots (c) Inhibition rate of S. aureus by different concentrations of carbon quantum dots (d)Inhibition of E. coli and S. aureus by CDs (e) Effect of different treatments on the cell wall of E. coli and S. aureus.

(a) Changes in the appearance of pears during 5 days of storage at 4 °C (b) Total bacterial count on the surface of fresh cut pear pulp tissue in different treatment groups (c) Phenolic content (d) Ascorbic acid (AsA) content (e) Glutathione (GSH) content (f) H2O2 content (g) O2 ·-content.
(a) Superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity (b) Catalase (CAT) activity (c) Peroxidase (POD) activity (d) Ascorbic acid oxidase (APX) activity (e) Polyphenol oxidase (PPO) activity.
Nanoparticle size and ζ-potential of different CDs.
Composition of elements analyzed by XPS.

head.ce:title: Nanocomposite coating based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots in extending shelf life of fresh-cut pears from the perspective of microorganism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, 266109, Qingdao, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, 266109, Qingdao, Shandong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qingdao Special Food Research Institute, 266109, Qingdao, Shandong, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qingdao Special Food Research Institute, 266109, Qingdao, Shandong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped quantum carbon dots were prepared based on Ethylenediaminetetra acetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-Na2) and citric acid, characterized for their structures and properties, and blended with chitosan to make nanocomposite coating film applied in the preservation of fresh-cut pears. The results showed that the PER-CDs (Pleurotus eryngii by-products carbon quantum dots), EDTANa2-doped PER carbon quantum dots (EPER-CDs), EDTANa2 and citric acid doped PER carbon quantum dots (E/NPER-CDs) prepared by hydrothermal method successfully introduced N–H groups on the surfaces, and the doped carbon dots showed good antibacterial on Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus and antioxidant properties. At the end of storage (5 d), the total bacterial count of fresh-cut pears coated with CS (chitosan)/2%E/NPER-CDs was 5.59 log CFU/g significantly lower than that of the control group (7.29 log CFU/g). The E/NPER-CDs inhibited the accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), delayed degradation of total phenol, ascorbic acid and glutathione, as well as significantly reduced the activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), polyphenol oxidase (POD), peroxidase (PPO), and ascorbic acid oxidase (APX) to achieve the effect of enhancing the quality of fresh-cut pears during storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The fresh cut fruit and vegetable industry is an important product in the modern living environment. Crown pears are juicy and sweet with acidity, rich in nutrients, containing a variety of vitamins and cellulose, with small core, high edible rate, and the firm texture made it more suitable for fresh-cut processing. However, during fresh-cutting, mechanical damage caused by processing such as peeling and cutting could destroy pear tissues (D. Wang et al., 2021), resulting in color browning (Li, Zhang, & Ge, 2017), fruit softening, loss of nutrients and aromatic odour. Moreover, these behaviors also promote the growth of microorganisms (Z. Chai, Zhang, Liu, Chen, & Meng, 2021), which accelerate the quality degradation process of fruit pulp. Therefore, safe, efficient, and low-cost fresh-cut fruits and vegetables preservation technology is an urgent topic to be developed for current production.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are nanomaterials with particle sizes smaller than 10 nm, discovered by chance in 2004 (Min, Ezati, & Rhim, 2022). In solution, they appear as spheroids composed of sp2-hybridised carbon atoms on the inside and sp3-hybridised carbon atoms on the outside (Zuo, Lu, Sun, Guo, & He, 2015), with abundant functional groups (mainly hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups) on the surface. During the study, CDs were found to have easy modifiability, microbial adhesion, diverse surface functionalities, excellent photostability and chemical stability (Rahmani & Ghaemy, 2019), good water solubility and biocompatibility (Lin et al., 2022). In addition, its antioxidant (Ji, Sheardy, Zeng, Zhang, Chevva, Allado, et al., 2019) and bacteriostatic activities (Sun, Wu, Zhang, Chu, Wang, Huang, et al., 2021), low toxicity (Dong, Li, Kang, Yang, Qu, Zhang, et al., 2021), and environmental friendliness (Moradi, Molaei, Kousheh, J, & McClements, 2023) make it a good candidate for use in food functional packaging (L. Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2022). The methods of preparing CDs and the use of different precursors to synthesize different types of CDs and apply them to different fields have become research hotspots. Carbon sources from food are a reliable option for low-cost and non-toxic preparation of CDs (Hashemi & Mousazadeh, 2021). It has been pointed out that carbon, hydrogen and oxygen are the main constituent elements of CDs, and their contents vary with synthesis conditions and precursors. CDs synthesized directly from a single precursor substance are characterized by a significant dependence on particle size and acidity, and lack of surface-specific functional groups to perform specific functions. CDs with specific functions can be developed by controlling particle size, chemical element composition, surface structure or introducing different groups (Y. Wu, Li, van der Mei, Busscher, & Ren, 2021). Due to the variable composition and structure of doped CDs, their typical properties, such as tunable fluorescence, chemical stability, non-toxicity, biocompatibility and easy modification, are gradually being explored (Yan et al., 2018). Heteroatom doping is an efficient way to modulate the properties of CDs. The synergistic effect of multiple heteroatom doping in the carbon lattice can instantly provide a high density of active centres and create new active sites to enhance catalytic activity (Miao et al., 2020). What's more, the strong interactions between heteroatom-doped CDs and surface functional groups can also effectively enhance their catalytic performance through accelerated intermolecular electron transfer. Nitrogen doping can modulate the local features of CDs, such as the charge dissipation regions between neighboring carbons (Das, Ganguly, Bose, Mondal, Choudhary, Gangopadhyay, et al., 2018). Elemental phosphorus and elemental sulphur doping can regulate the excited state density of CDs and increase the polarisability, structural defects and spin density of CDs. Co-doping of nitrogen, sulphur and phosphorus can provide improved carrier separation and thus increase peroxidase-like activity. (Tripathi, Ahn, Chung, Le, Saini, Bhati, et al., 2020).

The group's previous synthesized CDs using the by-products of Pleurotus eryngii(PER) by-products (PER-CDs), which can extend the shelf life of fresh-cut pears (Y. Wang, Qiu, Fang, Li, & Sun, 2023). However, the unit operations (washing, peeling, coring, cutting, etc.) used in the processing of fresh-cut pears destroy the surface cells and thus provide more nutrients to microorganisms than the whole fruit. As a result, fresh-cut pears become contaminated with foodborne pathogens during production, distribution and storage, posing a risk to the consumer health (Chai, Zhang, et al., 2021). The use of heteroatom doping to regulate the nature of CDs, through the control and introduction of different synthetic precursors and thus regulate the synthesis of CDs with particle size, composition of chemical elements, surface structure or containing different functional groups, so as to develop CDs with a specific function, in more and more studies doped with heteroatoms in CDs have been prepared, and increasingly used in antibacterial aspects (Saravanan, Das, Maruthapandi, Aryal, Michaeli, Mastai, et al., 2024).The principle of bacterial inhibition by CDs can be summarized as CDs adhere to bacterial surfaces by electrostatic action, induce ROS production, disrupt cell membranes and cause DNA/RNA damage (Dong, Liang, Meziani, Sun, & Yang, 2020)。Ethylenediaminetetra acetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-Na2) is a kind of food additive allowed to be added to food in China, which can play the role of color protection, retard food oxidation, rancidity and at the same time it is rich in nitrogen, carbon and oxygen. The permitted addition of EDTA-Na2 in China is 0.25 g/kg.The additions covered in this paper are within the permitted range. It is a very suitable nitrogen doped synthetic carbon precursor substance for the synthesis of CDs by nitrogen doping. Citric acid is a kind of natural antioxidant (Ounkaew, Kasemsiri, Kamwilaisak, Saengprachatanarug, Mongkolthanaruk, Souvanh, et al., 2018) and an ideal precursor for the synthesis of CDs. We wanted to endow CDs with antioxidant properties, so we fabricated CDs using citric acid as one of the doping materials. In this paper, two kinds of nitrogen-doped CDs were synthesized by using EDTA-Na2 and citric acid, and mixed with chitosan to prepare chitosan-coated film for preservation of sliced pears, with the aim of investigating its antibacterial activity and effect on browning of cut pears caused by reactive oxygen species (ROS).

The PER by-products and crown pears were purchased from Chengyang Fruit and Vegetable Wholesale Market, Qingdao City, Shandong Province. Chitosan (deacetylation >90%) purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). EDTA-Na2,citric acid and other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co.

CDs were prepared according to the synthesis method of Wang(Y. Wang et al., 2023) and Zhao et al. (L. Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2022). Briefly, PER-CDs were prepared in the same way as before (Y. Wang et al., 2023).

EDTANa2-doped PER carbon quantum dots (EPER-CDs) were prepared in the same way as PER-CDs by mixing 1.25 g PER by-product powder, 0.5 g EDTA-Na2 with ultrapure water (60 mL) by the hydrothermal method. EDTANa2 and citric acid doped PER carbon quantum dots (E/NPER-CDs) were prepared by mixing 1.25 g PER by-product powder, 0.5 g EDTA-Na2, 0.5 g citric acid and 60 mL ultrapure water. None of the above CDs are stored for more than 4 weeks.

The morphology of the three prepared CDs was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (HT7700, Hitachi, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 80 kV. The particle size and zeta potential of the CDs were determined using a nanoparticle size potentiostat (Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK). The FT-IR spectra of the CDs were recorded using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet islo, Thermo Ltd, USA). The spectral range was 4000-400 cm−1. XRD spectrum were obtained using a polycrystalline X-ray diffractometer (D8ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany) with the following experimental conditions: Cu Kα radiation, Ni filtering, power of 40 kV × 40 mA, scan range of 10°–80°, scan speed of 10°/min and recording interval of 0.01°. UV absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths were recorded using SpectraMax i3x zymography (Molecular Devices, Shanghai, China). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed on an ESCALAB 250 Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped CDs/chitosan coating solution
ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan (≥90% deacetylation) was prepared as a 1% (w/v) concentration chitosan solution by dissolving in 1% (v/v) acetic acid solution at 60 °C with stirring. Subsequently, PER-CDs, EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs powders were dissolved in 100 mg/mL of solution and filtered. The CDs solution was then added to the chitosan coating solution and stirred for 10 min. CS/2%(v/v) PER-CDs, CS/2%(v/v) EPER-CDs and CS/2%(v/v) E/NPER-CDs coating solutions with a concentration of 2% (v/v) PER-CDs, EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs were prepared.

DPPH scavenging was modeled according to Murru et al. (Murru, Badia-Laino, & Diaz-Garcia, 2020) with minor modifications. DPPH scavenging was tested by reacting DPPH-ethanol solution and different concentrations of CDs by thorough mixing for 30 min. Ethanol was used as a blank control instead of CDs solution and DPPH-ethanol solution (A0, A2). Measure the absorbance of each mixture (517 nm). The absorbance value of the CD solution sample was recorded as A1. The DPPH-scavenging activity of CDs was then obtained using formula (1):
(1)
DPPH
scavenging
activity
( % )
=
[ 1 −
(
A 1
−
A 2
)
/
A 0
]
× 100 %

The O2 ·- scavenging activity was determined according to the method of Fan et al. (Fan, Yang, Zhang, Li, & Bai, 2020) with modifications. O2 ·- was determined by mixing different concentrations of CDs solution with Tris-HCl buffer and o-triol solution, reacting at room temperature for 5 min, and finally adding hydrochloric acid solution to stop the reaction, and measuring the absorbance values using Tris-HCl buffer instead of CDs solution and o-triol solution as a blank control (A0, A2), respectively. And the absorbance value for the sample of CDs solution was recorded as A1. The O2 ·-scavenging activity of CDs was calculated using equation (2):
(2)
Superoxide
anion
removal
rate
( % )
=
[
A 0
−
(
A 1
−
A 2
)
]
/
A 0
× 100 %

The scavenging activity of CDs against hydroxyl radical (·OH) was determined based on the Fenton reaction, and the methodology is referred to Wu et al. (S. Wu, Qiu, Guo, Zhang, & Lvov, 2017). FeSO4 solution and ethanolic salicylic acid solution were mixed with CDs solution. Subsequently, H2O2 was added and distilled water was used to replace the sample of CDs solution and H2O2 respectively (A0, A2). The mixture was allowed to react for 30 min (37 °C) and the absorbance value was measured (510 nm); the absorbance value of the CDs solution sample was recorded as A1. The ·OH scavenging activity of the CDs was calculated using equation (3).
(3)
Hydroxyl
radical
scavenging
( % )
=
[
A 0
−
(
A 1
−
A 2
)
]
× 100 %

Concentration-dependent antimicrobial activity was determined with reference to the method of Zhao et al. (D. Zhao, Liu, Zhang, Huang, & Xiao, 2021). Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) were incubated with sterile LB (Luria Bertani) broth medium in an incubator at 37 °C for 4 h. The bacterial solution (106 CFU/mL) was mixed with the three kinds of CDs solutions (4, 2, 1, 0.5, 0.25, 0.125, and 0.06125 mg/mL).The mixture was incubated for 16–20 h and then the absorbance values(600 nm) were observed and the inhibition rate was calculated. The inhibition rate was calculated according to equation (4)
(4)
Inhibition
rate
( % )
=
O D
600 A
−
O D
600 B
−
O D
600 C
O D
600 A
−
O D
600 D
× 100 %
where OD600A is the absorbance value at 600 nm for the control group in which the bacterial solution was substituted for the drug solution; OD600B is the absorbance value at 600 nm for the experimental group; OD600C is the absorbance value at 600 nm for the different CDs solutions; and OD600D is the absorbance value at 600 nm for the control group in which the LB broth was substituted for the bacterial solution.

The antimicrobial resistance was determined by the method of Gao et al. (Gao et al., 2021) with slight modifications. The bacterial solution (106 CFU/mL) was mixed with 2 mg/mL of CDs solution and incubated at a constant temperature of 37 °C with shaking, and then spread evenly on the surface of an agar medium. The bacteria were incubated in a constant temperature incubator (37 °C, 24 h) and the bacterial inhibition ability was observed.

The morphology of bacteria exposed to CDs was assessed by SEM, using the method of Chai et al. (S. Chai, Zhou, Pei, Zhu, & Chen, 2021), slightly modified. CDs solution was mixed with bacterial solution (108 CFU/mL) for incubation (37 °C, 12 h). The bacterial solution was centrifuged at 2500 rpm for 5 min and the precipitate was washed and resuspended in PBS (phosphate buffered saline) buffer. The suspension was fixed with 2% glutaraldehyde. The fixed bacterial suspension was fixed with osmic acid for 1 h, washed with PBS buffer and then later dried with a gradient of 30 %, 50%, 70 %, 90 % and 100 % ethanol. The bacteria were dried, sprayed with gold and visualized using a scanning electron microscope.
The MTT was measured using the previous method (Y. Wang et al., 2023). Cytotoxicity of PER-CDs was evaluated using in vitro studies with Human Keratinocytes Cells (HaCaT).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-doped CDs/chitosan coating solution in fresh-cut pear storage
Changes in the appearance of fresh-cut pears
ce:section.ce:para: Uniform crown pears were cut into small slices and soaked for 2 min in coating solutions, including PER-CDs, EPER-CDs, and E/NPER-CDs, as well as distilled water, before being removed to dry and stored at 4 °C. The effect of CDs on preserving freshness was measured by monitoring changes in the appearance and color of the fresh-cut pears.

15 g of fresh-cut pear pulp from each treatment group was added to 150 mL of 0.9 % saline, followed by oscillation for 3 min. The oscillated solution was then diluted 10-fold to produce a multi-concentration dilution. 1 mL of the dilution was taken and inoculated onto the agar plate, and the plate was then rotated to ensure homogeneity. Sterile saline was used instead of the dilution for the blank control. Plates were incubated for 48 h at constant temperature of 28 °C and colonies were counted and expressed as log CFU/g.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of antioxidant substances in fresh-cut pears
ce:section.ce:para: Determination of ascorbic acid in freshly cut pear samples by the method of Schiassi(Schiassi, Souza, Lago, Campos, & Queiroz, 2018), expressed as mg/100g.

Total phenol content was determined with reference to the method of Wei et al. (Wei & Zhao, 2020), and the results were expressed as the total phenolic content per gram of freshly cut pear flesh at an absorbance of 280 nm, i.e. OD280/g. Glutathione was determined using the method provided by Nanjing Jiancheng Kit, and the content was expressed as mg/kg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ROS content in fresh-cut pears
ce:section.ce:para: The H2O2 and O2 ·- content was determined by the method of Tang et al.(Tang et al., 2021) and Ge et al. (Ge, Li, Cui, Zhang, Zhang, Zhang, et al., 2019) with slight modifications. Moreover, the Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute Hydrogen Peroxide kit was employed to determine the H2O2 content and O2 ·- content in the fresh-cut pear tissues. H2O2 can interact with molybdic acid to form a complex. The amount of H2O2 can be calculated by measuring the amount of complex at 405 nm. O2 ·- production was measured by modeling the xanthine and xanthine oxidase reaction system in the organism. Xanthine oxidase oxidizes hydroxylamine to form nitrite, which appears purplish-red in the presence of a colorant. The absorbance was measured.
The H2O2 content was reported as nmol g−1 FW. The final O2 ·- content expressed as nmol g−1 FW min−1, was calculated using 0.15 g of freshly cut pear pulp tissue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of enzyme activities related to ROS metabolism
ce:section.ce:para: The levels of superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), polyphenol oxidase (POD), peroxidase (PPO), and ascorbic acid oxidase (APX) were measured in freshly cut pear pulp tissues using the kit method for Nanjing Jianjian Bioengineering Institute. Similar to the principle of O2 ·-measurement, SOD has a specific inhibitory effect on O2 ·-. So that the formation of nitrite is reduced. And the SOD activity in the measured sample can be found out through the measurement of absorbance.The decomposition of H2O2 by CAT can be rapidly aborted by the addition of ammonium molybdate. The remaining H2O2 interacts with the ammonium molybdate to produce a yellowish complex. The change of which is measured at 405 nm, and the activity of CAT can be calculated.Using the principle of POD-catalyzed H2O2 reaction, the enzyme activity was determined by measuring the change of absorbance at 420 nm.PPO is able to catalyze the production of quinone from the substrate phenol. Quinone has characteristic light absorption at 420 nm.APX catalyzes the reaction of ascorbic acid (AsA) with H2O2 to oxidize AsA to monodehydroascorbic acid. As AsA is oxidized, the absorbance value at 290 nm in the solution decreases. APX activity was calculated based on the decrease in absorbance value per unit time. The results were expressed as U/mg−1.

Graphs were generated using the software Origin2023b. Experimental data underwent one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and presented as means and standard deviations. Significant differences between means were determined using Duncan's new multivariate extreme variance test at the 95 % confidence level. Statistical calculations were performed using SPSS 26. Unless stated otherwise, all the experiments were repeated three times.

As shown in the TEM images (Fig. 1 a–c), the CDs prepared from PER by-products were uniformly spherical and well dispersed. The particle size distributions and ζ-potentials of the three CDs were shown in Table 1 . The average particle sizes of PER-CDs, EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs were 2.89 ± 1.27 nm, 3.27 ± 1.53 nm and 6.4 ± 3.7 nm, respectively. The results showed that all of them had particle sizes less than 10 nm, which was in the range of the typical particle size distribution of CDs (Priyadarshi, Riahi, Rhim, Han, & Lee, 2021). The particle sizes of EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs synthesized after doping are larger than those of PER-CDs and are significantly different (P < 0.05). This may be due to the fact that EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs have more functional groups on their surface. The ζ potential of the nanoparticles corresponds to the surface charge density of the particles, which can be used to indicate the stability of the nanoparticle solution. In general, the higher the ζ potential, the more stable the nanoparticle solution; at the same time, the ζ value is a condition for possible interactions between CDs and bacteria. Therefore, the ζ potentials of the three CDs were measured as shown in Table 1, the ζ-potential of the PER-CDs was −14.63 ± 0.83 mV, and after doping with EDTA-Na2, the ζ-potential decreased to −20.17 ± 1.56 mV, which can be attributed to the fact that nitrogen atoms (3.04) have higher electronegativity than carbon atoms (2.55) (Xu, Wu, & Xu, 2007). However, after doping with EDTA-Na2 and citric acid, the ζ-potential was increased to −6.29 ± 0.24 mV, which may be due to the fact that citric acid is mainly composed of carbon atoms, leading to an increase in its ζ-potential.

The particle size distributions and ζ potentials of the three CDs show significant variability, which may be caused by the different types of surface functional groups. Functional groups at the surface of the three CDs were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. The CDs in Fig. 1d have simultaneously a broad peak at 3278.04 cm−1 and relatively narrow peaks at 2917.92 cm−1, 1591.31 cm−1, 1403.56 cm−1 and 1024.97 cm−1. These may correspond to the O–H stretching vibrational peaks, C–H stretching vibrational peaks, CO and C–O stretching vibrational peaks and CC stretching bending vibrational peaks. Based on these peaks, it is hypothesized that carboxyl and hydroxyl groups may be present on the surface of CDs. The presence of these functional groups makes the PER-CDs, EPER-CDs, E/NPER-CDs hydrophilic and water soluble. EDTA-Na2 doped EPER-CDs have two peaks that are more pronounced compared to PER-CDs, which are 1622 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 respectively. These two peaks may be more pronounced than those of PER-CDs, which could correspond to the bending vibration of N–H and the stretching vibration of C–N, respectively (Tripathi, et al., 2020). These results indicate that nitrogen has been successfully introduced to the surface of EPER-CDs and present in the form of an amine group. E/NPER-CDs also showed two peaks similar to those of EPER-CDs and a C–N stretching vibrational peak at 1200 cm−1.

The UV absorption spectra (Fig. 1e) showed the ability of the three CDs to absorb UV light. E/NPER-CDs have better ability to absorb UV light. Therefore, E/NPER-CDs have good application prospects in retarding food oxidation. The constituent elements and chemical structure of the CDs were determined by XPS. Fig. 1f showed that the CDs have three main peaks: 284.8 eV (C 1 s), 399 eV (N 1 s), 531Ev (O 1 s). This indicates that all CDs are composed of C, N, and O elements. As shown in Table 2 , the content of C was the highest, and this result indicated that the constituent elements of the CDs were mainly C. In addition, the N content of the E/NPER-CDs was higher than that of the two outer CDs, indicating that N doping has been successfully carried out. The XRD patterns of the three CDs were shown in Fig. 1g. The CDs showed a diffraction peak around 2 θ = 20°, indicating that the CDs have a graphite-like structure (Liu et al., 2017). The effect of the excitation wavelength on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was shown in Fig. 1h–j. The fluorescence peaks were red-shifted with the change of excitation wavelength, the peak intensity shows an increasing and then decreasing trend, and the highest fluorescence intensity was observed at 375 nm excitation wavelength. This phenomenon indicates a narrower particle size distribution or fewer energy paths (Mei, Fu, Su, Chen, Lin, Zheng, et al., 2022). In addition, the prepared CDs also showed better stability at different pH. From Figs. S1–3, it can be seen that all three CDs can maintain stable fluorescence intensity at different pH.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs showed significant improvement in the scavenging ability of ·OH and O2 ·- compared to PER-CDs. In particular, the scavenging rate of O2 ·- was enhanced by 30.3 % and 41.82 % respectively. This result indicated that EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs had stronger electron donating ability, which might be related to the introduction of more amine groups. 2 mg/mL of PER-CDs, EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs scavenges DPPH by more than 90 %, and the scavenging ability for hydroxyl radicals was improved. This suggests that the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surfaces, which can provide hydrogen atoms and thus scavenge both DPPH and hydroxyl radicals are still present and have more active sites.

Testing the concentration-dependent antimicrobial activity of substances against bacteria is a standard method for investigating their antimicrobial activity. Two food-borne pathogenic bacteria, Gram-positive (S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli), were selected as strains to test the concentration-dependent antimicrobial activity of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2b-c, CDs have different antimicrobial activities and the same CDs have different inhibitory activities for different types of bacteria. Among them, PER-CDs showed no inhibitory effect on two types of bacteria, while the doped EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs had higher inhibitory activities than PER-CDs, which were positively correlated with the concentration. The inhibition rate of 2 mg/mL of EPER-CDs on two types of bacteria had reached 60.66 ± 3.43 % (S. aureus) and 56.58 ± 0.85 % (E. coli), and E/NPER-CDs inhibited up to 92.36 % ± 0.95 % (S. aureus) and 85.15 ± 2.43 % (E. coli).

To further evaluate the antimicrobial activity of CDs, the inhibitory effect of the same concentration of CDs (2 mg/mL) on S. aureus and E. coli was evaluated by plate coating, using sterile water as a control. As shown in Fig. 2d, 2 mg/mL of PER-CDs showed almost no inhibitory effect on E. coli and S. aureus, and EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs solutions showed significant bacteriostatic effect on S. aureus and E. coli. The results showed that the CDs were endowed with stronger antibacterial effects after doping. The antibacterial activity of EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs was stronger against S. aureus (gram-positive bacteria) than against E. coli (gram-negative bacteria), which was mainly due to the differences in the structure of the bacterial cell wall and the interaction force with the CDs, which made it easier for them to penetrate into E. coli.

Morphological changes in E. coli and S. aureus treated with PER-CDs, EPER-CDs, E/NPER-CDs were observed using SEM to investigate the antimicrobial effect of CDs at the microscopic level. There were clear differences between untreated and treatment with PER-CDs in S. aureus, E. coli, and in those treated with EPER-CDs/E/NPER-CDs. SEM images of untreated and PER-CDs-treated E. coli and S. aureus showed the smooth surface characteristics of normal bacteria. Conversely, bacteria treated with EPER-CDs/E/NPER-CDs exhibited disrupted membranes, irregular cellular morphology, membrane invagination, and even fragmented bacterial membranes (Fig. 2e). These observations suggest that EPER-CDs/E/NPER-CDs can terminate bacterial growth by impeding their cell membranes. This might be due to the amino group and amide bond present on the surface of the nitrogen-doped CDs, which significantly contribute to the boosted antibacterial function. The protonated state of this group creates an electrostatic impact with the phospholipids of the bacterial somatic cell membrane, leading to harm to the membrane, leak out of bacterial cytoplasm, and eventual bacterial demise. Although the precise function of CDs in antibacterial activity remains unclear, Mei et al. (Mei, Fu, Su, Chen, Lin, Zheng, et al., 2022) have proposed a possible explanation. The small size of CDs is believed to be the cause of their antimicrobial properties, as they can penetrate bacterial cell membranes quickly and impact cellular metabolism.

From the results of MTT (Fig. S4), none of the three CDs at low concentrations showed significant cytotoxicity. At the concentration applied for fresh-cut pear preservation (2 mg/mL), there was no significant decrease in the survival of HaCaT. CDs higher than 2.5 mg/mL showed more significant cytotoxicity. Therefore, all three CDs can be used in food preservation applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-doped CDs/chitosan coating in fresh-cut pear
Changes in the appearance of fresh-cut pears
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the efficacy of CDs/chitosan coating in prolonging the shelf life of fresh-cut pears, a 5-day experiment was conducted (Fig. 3 a). On the 3rd day of storage, the samples in the control group (CK) presented wrinkles. Both the CK and PER-CDs groups exhibited more severe browning, coupled with volume reduction. The CS/2%E/NPER-CDs and CS/2%EPER-CDs groups exhibited significant browning by the 5th day. However, the volume reduction in the CS/2%EPER-CDs treatment group was more pronounced. The aforementioned findings indicate that the use of CS/2%EPER-CDs treatment significantly increases the shelf-life of sliced pears.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in the total bacterial count of fresh-cut pears
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3b showed the effect of various treatments on the total bacterial count on the surface of sliced pears pulp tissue. As depicted in the figure, the total bacterial count on the samples gradually increased during storage across all treatment groups. In particular, the total bacterial count observed on samples treated with CS/2%EPER-CDs and CS/2%E/NPER-CDs were significantly lower than those observed on control and CS/PER-CDs treated samples. Particularly notable was that on day 0, the total bacterial count of CS/2%EPER-CDs and CS/2%E/NPER-CDs treated fresh-cut pears were 3.96 logarithmic colony forming units per gram (log CFU/g) and 3.79 log CFU/g, respectively, which were significantly lower than those of the control (4.46 log CFU/g). By day 4 of storage, total bacterial count in the control group had already risen to 6.56 log CFU/g, exceeding the minimum food safety limit of 6 log CFU/g for food. At the conclusion of storage, the total bacterial count for samples treated with CS/2%EPER-CDs and CS/2%E/NPER-CDs were 5.87 log CFU/g and 5.59 log CFU/g, respectively. The total bacterial count for the group treated with CS/2%E/NPER-CDs was notably lower than the other groups at the end of storage, suggesting successful inhibition of microorganism growth in fresh-cut pears following treatment with CS/2%EPER-CDs and CS/2%E/NPER-CDs. The total bacterial count for the group treated with CS/2%E/NPER-CDs was notably lower than the other groups at the end of storage, suggesting successful inhibition of microorganism growth in cut pears following treatment with CS/2%EPER-CDs and CS/2%E/NPER-CDs. Importantly, CS/2%E/NPER-CDs treatment demonstrated superior effectiveness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different treatments on antioxidant content of fresh-cut pears
ce:section.ce:para: Phenolics, AsA and glutathione (GSH) were the primary antioxidants with ROS-scavenging potential in crown pear fruit. The antioxidants demonstrated a diminishing trend towards the end of storage of freshly cut pears, as illustrated in Fig. 3c–e.

The fresh-cut pears' polyphenol content in various treatment groups (Fig. 3c) exhibited an upward and downward trend, and was consistently higher in the treatment group as opposed to the control group throughout storage. On one hand, the mechanical damage to freshly cut pears during processing may generate secondary metabolic reactions. Phenolics are the most widely occurring secondary metabolites in plants. On the other hand, oxidized phenolics can be reduced to phenolics again under the reducing effect of ascorbic acid. Therefore, the total phenolic content of different treatment groups at the beginning of storage also showed an increasing trend and maintained a relatively high level. After five days of storage, the total phenolic content in the CS/2% E/NPER-CDs coated treatment group was 0.28 OD280/g. This amount was 1.4 times higher than that of the control group. These results confirm that including PER-CDs, EPER-CDs, E/NPER-CDs can enhance the retention of phenolics in fresh-cut pears while stored. Moreover, the addition of E/NPER-CDs onto chitosan coating can effectively slow down the depletion of phenolics sliced pears during storage.

AsA is a crucial nutrient in fresh-cut pears, and its content alteration during storage of fresh-cut fruits and vegetables is an essential preservation indicator. The AsA content was observed to decrease during storage of these pears (Fig. 3d), which could be attributed to the involvement of AsA in the quinone reduction process. Particularly, the control group demonstrated the most significant and speediest decline, with the ascorbic acid content decreasing by 68.1 % after five days of storage. In contrast, the CS/2% E/NPER-CDs coated group only experienced a decrease of 39.5 % by the end of storage. These findings indicate that the CS/2%E/NPER-CDs coating has the capability to efficiently reduce the loss of ascorbic acid from sliced pears.

As illustrated in Fig. 3e, the GSH levels in fresh-cut pears across various treatment groups exhibited a declining trend. Notably, the GSH content in the CS/2% PER-CDs, CS/2%EPER-CDs and CS/2%E/NPER-CDs coated treatment groups was significantly (P < 0.05) higher than that of the control group. At 5 days of storage, the GSH content in the control group was only found in CS/PER-CDs. However, the treatment groups coated in CS/EPER-CDs and CS/E/NPER-CDs exhibited GSH levels of 78.64 %, 72.78 % and 70.67 %, respectively. Among them, the coating of CS/2%E/NPER-CDs was the most efficient in limiting the reduction of GSH content in fresh-cut pears.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different treatments on H2O2 and O2 ·-content of fresh-cut pears
ce:section.ce:para: The excessive accumulation of H2O2 and O2 ·- would cause peroxidation of cell membrane lipids in fresh-cut pears thereby accelerating the senescence browning process. The impact of various treatments on the H2O2 and O2 ·- content of sliced pears were shown in Fig. 3f–g. Both the H2O2 and O2 ·- content showed a trend of increasing and then decreasing. At 0 d of storage, the H2O2 and O2 ·- content of fresh-cut pears coated with CS/2%EPER-CDs and CS/2%E/NPER-CDs were significantly lower than those of the control group, which may be attributed to the roles played by EPER-CDs and E/NPER-CDs in scavenging H2O2 and O2 ·-. The CS/2%EPER-CDs and CS/2%E/NPER-CDs coated treatments delayed the emergence of the peak levels of H2O2 and O2 ·- contents of samples and reduced these levels throughout storage, with the CS/2%E/NPER-CDs coated treatment being the most effective. Therefore, it can be concluded that the film coated with CS/2%E/NPER-CDs was successful in inhibiting the accumulation of free radicals during storage and improved the quality of fresh-cut pears.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The enzymes activities related to ROS metabolism
ce:section.ce:para: SOD and CAT, two essential antioxidant enzymes, occur in pear crowns, and the elimination of ROS in the pear's direct antioxidant system is primarily associated with antioxidant enzyme activity. SOD converts and O2 ·- to H2O2, while CAT disintegrates H2O2 into H2O and CO2. POD, an oxidative enzyme belonging to a class present in pears, acts in conjunction with SOD and CAT. Furthermore, it can serve as a physiological indicator of tissue senescence. APX catalyzes the formation of water from ascorbic acid and hydrogen peroxide. PPO is a metalloprotease that oxidizes phenolics to quinones in pear pulp tissue.

As depicted in Fig. 4 a and b, the SOD and CAT activities in the pulp tissue of sliced pears from different treatment groups exhibited an increasing and then decreasing trend during the storage period. The SOD and CAT activities were reduced to varying degrees in the CS/2%PER-CDs, CS/2%EPER-CDs, and CS/2%E/NPER-CDs coated treatments compared to the CK group, with the CS/2%E/NPER-CDs coated treatment delaying the peak arrival of SOD and CAT enzyme activities. Until the 4th d of storage, the SOD activity of the CS/2%E/NPER-CDs coated group reached 87.82 U/mg, which was only 86.5 % of the peak value of the control group. In the control group, a rapid increase in O2 ·-content resulted in a rapid increase in SOD activity. O2 ·- content and SOD activity both reached peak values on the second day of storage. On the contrary, in the group coated with CS/2%E/NPER-CDs, the process of O2 ·- accumulation was decelerated possibly because of the E/NPER-CDs' scavenging capacity of free radicals. The content of O2 ·- reached its peak on the 3rd day of storage while SOD activity peaked on the 4th day. The findings from various treatments on fresh-cut pear flesh demonstrated a swift increase in O2 ·- content which resulted in the SOD activity increase.

Fig. 4c–d showed the trends in POD and APX activities in fresh-cut pear, subject to various treatments. The activities of POD and APX in the different treatment groups were first increased and then decreased during the storage period, while the activity of PPO (Fig. 4e) displayed an upward trend. However, the POD, APX and PPO activities of the groups treated with CS/2%PER-CDs, CS/2%EPER-CDs and CS/2%E/NPER-CDs were significantly lower than those of the CK group. The group coated with the CS/2%E/NPER-CDs coated had the lowest POD, APX and PPO activities. This may be attributed to the strong oxidizing activity of E/NPER-CDs, which effectively scavenges some free radicals. Additionally, the formation of a chitosan film on the surface of freshly cut pears obstructs oxygen and protects the membrane structure, thereby reducing the accumulation of harmful O2 ·- and H2O2. This results in the inhibition of activities of POD, APX and PPO as reported by Batista Silva et al. (Batista Silva, Cosme Silva, Santana, Salvador, Medeiros, Belghith, et al., 2018).

During the latter storage period, POD and APX exhibited a greater activity, thereby demonstrating a more effective ability to scavenge free radicals. This constructive attribute is beneficial in preserving the overall storage quality of fresh-cut pears.

It can be seen that CS/2%E/NPER-CDs coating film can significantly reduce the activities of SOD, CAT, POD, APX and PPO in fresh-cut pears and delay the arrival of the peaks of activities of SOD, CAT, POD and APX. The results demonstrated that CS/2%E/NPER-CDs coating could effectively delay the accumulation level of O2 ·- and H2O2, thus reducing the oxidative damage of fruit by reactive oxygen species and achieving the freshness preservation effect.

CDs with antioxidant and antimicrobial activity were prepared by hydrothermal method using PER by-products as precursors and co-doping with EDTANa2 and citric acid. The prepared CDs were mixed with chitosan to prepare the coating film. The coating solution was applied to the storage of fresh-cut pears. The CS/2%E/NPER-CDs coating treatment could significantly inhibit the generation and accumulation of ROS compared with the CK group, and delay the degradation of antioxidant substances in fresh-cut pears. At the same time, the activity of enzymes involved in the generation of ROS was significantly reduced and the quality of sliced pears was significantly improved during storage. This indicates that E/NPER-CDs are effective in improving the quality of fresh-cut pears and have a wide range of application.

WeiYu Qiu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Yihao Wang: Resources, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenxiang Li: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fansheng Cheng: Supervision. XingFeng Xu: Supervision. Yanan Sun: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Shandong Province Modern Agricultural Industry Technology System (SDAIT-07-08), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022QC104), the Breeding Plan of Shandong Provincial Qingchuang Research Team (Innovation Team of Functional Plant Protein-Based Food).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Sun et al., 2021
